content:
# Acts Chapter 13 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 240-242 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_013_page_001.jpg, ACT_013_page_002.jpg, ACT_013_page_003.jpg

---

**1** Now there were in Antioch, in the church that was there, prophets and teachers: Barnabas, and Simeon who was called Niger, and Lucius the Cyrenian, and Manaen who had been brought up with Herod the tetrarch, and Saul.

**2** And as they were ministering to the Lord and fasting, the Holy Spirit said, "Set apart for me Barnabas and Saul for the work to which I have called them."

**3** Then having fasted and prayed and laid their hands on them, they sent them away.

**4** So they, having been sent out by the Holy Spirit, went down to Seleucia, and from there they sailed to Cyprus.

**5** And when they were in Salamis, they proclaimed the word of God in the synagogues of the Jews. And they also had John as a minister.

**6** And having gone through the whole island as far as Paphos, they found a certain man, a magician, a Jewish false prophet, whose name was Bar-Jesus,

**7** who was with the proconsul Sergius Paulus, an intelligent man. This man summoned Barnabas and Saul and sought to hear the word of God.

**8** But Elymas the magician -- for so his name is translated -- opposed them, seeking to turn the proconsul away from the faith.

**9** But Saul, who is also Paul, having been filled with the Holy Spirit, looked intently at him

**10** and said, "O you who are full of all deceit and all fraud, son of the devil, enemy of all righteousness, will you not cease perverting the straight ways of the Lord?

**11** And now, behold, the hand of the Lord is upon you, and you shall be blind, not seeing the sun for a time." And immediately mist and darkness fell upon him, and going about he sought those who would lead him by the hand.

**12** Then the proconsul, having seen what had happened, believed, being astonished at the teaching of the Lord.

**13** And having set sail from Paphos, those around Paul came to Perga of Pamphylia. But John, having departed from them, returned to Jerusalem.

**14** And they, having passed through from Perga, came to Antioch of Pisidia, and having gone into the synagogue on the day of the Sabbath, they sat down.

**15** And after the reading of the Law and the Prophets, the rulers of the synagogue sent to them, saying, "Men, brothers, if there is in you any word of exhortation for the people, speak."

**16** And Paul, having stood up and motioning with his hand, said: "Men of Israel, and you who fear God, listen.

**17** The God of this people Israel chose our fathers and exalted the people during their sojourn in the land of Egypt, and with an uplifted arm he led them out of it.

**18** And for a period of about forty years he endured them in the wilderness.

**19** And having destroyed seven nations in the land of Canaan, he gave them their land as an inheritance

**20** for about four hundred and fifty years. And after these things he gave them judges until Samuel the prophet.

**21** And from that time they asked for a king, and God gave them Saul the son of Kish, a man of the tribe of Benjamin, for forty years.

**22** And having removed him, he raised up David to be their king, to whom he also said, bearing witness, 'I have found David the son of Jesse, a man after my heart, who will do all my will.'

**23** From this man's seed, according to promise, God brought to Israel a Savior, Jesus,

**24** after John had first proclaimed, before the face of his coming, a baptism of repentance to all the people of Israel.

**25** And as John was completing his course, he said, 'What do you suppose that I am? I am not he. But behold, one comes after me, the sandals of whose feet I am not worthy to loose.'

**26** Men, brothers, sons of the race of Abraham, and those among you who fear God, to us the word of this salvation has been sent.

**27** For those who dwell in Jerusalem and their rulers, not having known him, and the voices of the prophets that are read every Sabbath, fulfilled them by condemning him.

**28** And though they found no cause of death, they asked Pilate that he should be put to death.

**29** And when they had fulfilled all the things that had been written concerning him, they took him down from the tree and laid him in a tomb.

**30** But God raised him from the dead,

**31** and he appeared for many days to those who had come up with him from Galilee to Jerusalem, who are now his witnesses to the people.

**32** And we proclaim to you the good news of the promise made to the fathers,

**33** that God has fulfilled this to us, their children, having raised up Jesus, as it is also written in the second Psalm, 'You are my Son; today I have begotten you.'

**34** And that he raised him from the dead, no longer about to return to corruption, he has spoken thus: 'I will give you the holy and sure things of David.'

**35** Therefore he also says in another place, 'You will not allow your Holy One to see corruption.'

**36** For David, having served the purpose of God in his own generation, fell asleep and was laid with his fathers and saw corruption,

**37** but he whom God raised up did not see corruption.

**38** Therefore let it be known to you, men and brothers, that through this one forgiveness of sins is proclaimed to you,

**39** and from all things from which you could not be justified by the law of Moses, in this one everyone who believes is justified.

**40** Therefore take heed, lest that which was spoken in the prophets should come upon you:

**41** 'Look, you scoffers, and marvel, and perish, for I am working a work in your days, a work which you will by no means believe, even if someone declares it to you.'"

**42** And as they were going out, they urged that these words might be spoken to them on the next Sabbath.

**43** And when the synagogue had been dismissed, many of the Jews and the devout proselytes followed Paul and Barnabas, who, speaking to them, were persuading them to continue in the grace of God.

**44** And on the coming Sabbath, nearly the whole city was gathered together to hear the word of the Lord.

**45** But when the Jews saw the crowds, they were filled with jealousy and were contradicting the things spoken by Paul, blaspheming.

**46** And speaking boldly, Paul and Barnabas said, "It was necessary that the word of God should be spoken to you first. Since you thrust it away and judge yourselves unworthy of eternal life, behold, we turn to the Gentiles.

**47** For so the Lord has commanded us: 'I have set you as a light of the Gentiles, that you should be for salvation to the end of the earth.'"

**48** And hearing this, the Gentiles were rejoicing and glorifying the word of the Lord, and as many as had been appointed to eternal life believed.

**49** And the word of the Lord was being spread throughout the whole region.

**50** But the Jews stirred up the devout women of prominence and the leading men of the city, and they raised up persecution against Paul and Barnabas, and cast them out from their borders.

**51** And they, having shaken off the dust of their feet against them, came to Iconium.

**52** And the disciples were being filled with joy and with the Holy Spirit.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**Textual Observations from Codex Sinaiticus:**

1. **Verse 1 (Prophets and teachers):** The Sinaiticus text lists the five leaders at Antioch. The name Manaen is rendered with the characteristic Sinaiticus spelling. The phrase "brought up with" (syntrophos) indicates a foster-brother or childhood companion of Herod Antipas.

2. **Verse 5 (John as minister):** Sinaiticus reads "hypereten" (minister/attendant), confirming John Mark served in a subordinate assisting role rather than as a co-missionary.

3. **Verse 8 (Elymas):** The parenthetical explanation "for so his name is translated" (houtOs gar methermEneuetai to onoma autou) is present in Sinaiticus. The etymological connection between "Elymas" and "magician" remains debated among scholars.

4. **Verse 13 (Paul's company):** Sinaiticus reads "hoi peri Paulon" (those around Paul), marking the transition of leadership from Barnabas to Paul. From this point forward, Paul is consistently named first.

5. **Verse 18 (etropophoresEn):** Sinaiticus reads "etropophoresEn" (he endured/bore with them), rather than the variant "etrophophoresEn" (he nourished/cared for them) found in some manuscripts. The difference is a single letter but significantly changes the theological emphasis -- divine patience versus divine nurture.

6. **Verse 19-20 (Chronological note):** The "four hundred and fifty years" in Sinaiticus aligns with the Western text tradition, covering the period of the patriarchs, sojourn, and conquest, rather than the period of the judges alone.

7. **Verse 23 (Savior):** Sinaiticus reads "Sotera Iesoun" (a Savior, Jesus). This formal title emphasizes the messianic fulfillment from David's line.

8. **Verse 33 (Second Psalm):** Sinaiticus specifies "the second Psalm" (tO psalmO tO deuterO), a reading preserved in the major uncials. Paul's sermon applies Psalm 2:7 to the resurrection, not the eternal generation of the Son.

9. **Verse 34 (Holy things of David):** The quotation from Isaiah 55:3 LXX, "ta hosia Dauid ta pista," rendered "the holy and sure things of David," refers to the covenant promises now fulfilled in Christ's resurrection.

10. **Verse 41 (Habakkuk quotation):** The citation of Habakkuk 1:5 follows the LXX text. Sinaiticus reads "kataphronetai" (scoffers/despisers), consistent with the Septuagint tradition rather than the Masoretic "among the nations."

11. **Verse 44 (Word of the Lord):** Sinaiticus reads "ton logon tou kyriou" (the word of the Lord), whereas some manuscripts read "ton logon tou theou" (the word of God). This is a theologically significant variant.

12. **Verse 48 (Appointed to eternal life):** The verb "tetagmenoi" (having been appointed/ordained) is a perfect passive participle, preserving the divine passive -- those who believed were those whom God had appointed to eternal life. This is a key text for the doctrine of election.

**Translation Philosophy:** This rendering maintains strict formal equivalence with the Greek of Sinaiticus while ensuring comprehensible English. Participial chains, common in Lukan Greek, have been preserved where possible (e.g., "having fasted and prayed and laid their hands on them"). Paul's synagogue sermon (vv. 16-41) follows the rhetorical structure of the Greek, with its historical recital, christological proclamation, and prophetic warning intact.

-- LOGOS
